mappings.rationaleAI: Core text for this section: covers weathering (A.1), mass movement (A.3), wind erosion (A.5), sea level change (A.6), and landform evolution concepts (D).
Covers weathering, erosion, sediment transport, and the geological processes that underpin geomorphic landscape change across all environments.
Directly covers fluvial processes (A.4), drainage patterns (C.1.b), river floodplains and deltas (C.1.d), and the fluvial environment (C.1).
Covers the lacustrine environment (C.2) including lake basins, lake hydraulics (C.2.b), sedimentation (A.8.b), and floodplain lakes.
Directly covers the desert environment (C.5) including eolian processes (A.5), sand dunes, desert landforms (C.5.b), and desert boundaries (C.5.c).
Core text for glacial processes (A.7) and the glacial environment (C.6): covers erosional landforms like cirques and fjords (C.6.b), moraines, drumlins (C.6.c), and sedimentology.
Covers glacial and periglacial processes (C.6.d), permafrost, ice sheet landforms, and the Quaternary landscape modifications discussed in the glacial environment.
Covers marine processes (A.6) including coastal erosion and deposition, sediment transport by density flows (A.6.b), and the continental shelf (C.4.d).
Relevant to coral reefs as coastal depositional features (C.4.a.iv) within the marine environment.
Covers mountain geomorphology including glacial erosion features (C.6.b), periglacial processes (C.6.d), natural hazards related to slope processes (A.3), and alpine valleys.
Directly relevant to volcanic-tectonic processes (B.3) that create and modify surface features including lava flows and pyroclastic deposits.
Covers the polar environment (C.7) including permafrost (C.6.d.i), periglacial landforms, and cryospheric processes shaping Arctic landscapes.
Covers the polar environment (C.7) with glacial processes, ice sheet dynamics, and polar landform features.
Relevant to the tropical rain forest (C.8) and temperate woodland (C.9) environments, and the role of vegetation in geomorphic processes (B.1.b).
Covers tropical grassland environments and geomorphic processes in savannas, relevant to the tropical environment (C.8) and vegetation effects on landscape.
Climate drives weathering rates (A.1), controls erosion and deposition patterns, and determines the morphogenetic regions (D.3) that characterize different environments.
Relevant to weathering processes (A.1) acting on different rock types, sedimentary rock formation from erosion products, and bedrock-dependent coastal features (C.4.c).
Relevant to weathering processes (A.1) as mineral composition controls weathering rates, and to clay mineral formation in soils (A.2).
Covers chemical weathering (A.1), surface processes, and the interplay of Earth systems that drive geomorphic change.
Covers volcanic-tectonic processes (B.3), earthquakes triggering mass movements (A.3), flooding events shaping fluvial landforms (C.1.d), and storm-driven coastal erosion (A.6).
Relevant to volcanic-tectonic processes (B.3) and the tectonic framework underlying uniformitarianism (D.1) and landform evolution theory (D).
Covers human modification of the Earth's surface (B.1.c) through land cultivation, mining, construction, and other activities that alter geomorphic processes.
Directly relevant to soil erosion and deterioration (A.2.d), land cultivation effects on surface features (B.1.c), and soil formation processes.

mappings.vsiAuthor: John A. Matthews, David T. Herbert
mappings.rationaleAI: Covers physical geography including geomorphic processes, the concept of morphogenetic regions (D.3), and environmental characteristics of major landscape types.
Covers the interactions between hydrosphere, atmosphere, biosphere, and lithosphere (sections A and B) that collectively drive geomorphic processes.
Relevant to understanding landform evolution through geologic time (C.1.e, D) and the sedimentary record of past geomorphic environments.
Covers microbial roles in soil formation (A.2, B.1.a) including nitrogen fixation and biological weathering of rocks.
